steps.title: Identify the hypothesis's prediction
steps.content: The second team predicts that in low-visibility conditions with large vortices, intermediate-headed fish will be more likely than narrow-headed fish to detect obstructions. More likely to detect = fewer collisions. A supporting finding must compare both types in exactly those conditions and show intermediate-headed fish performing better.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura bumped into obstructions just as often as narrow-headed Selene vomer': equal performance — this contradicts the hypothesis rather than supporting it. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Narrow-headed S. vomer bumped into more than half the obstructions': only tests narrow-headed fish; we learn nothing about how intermediate-headed fish compare, so the hypothesis is unaddressed. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Narrow-headed S. vomer bumped into obstructions more often than intermediate-headed L. rhabdoura': directly compares both fish types in the specified conditions and shows intermediate-headed fish detecting better (fewer collisions) — exactly what the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Some intermediate-headed L. rhabdoura bumped into obstructions more than other specimens of the same fish': measures within-species variation only; no comparison to narrow-headed fish, so the hypothesis is untested. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
